briefing.headline: 10 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: European Journal of Emergency Medicine, MedPage Today Emergency Medicine, REBEL EM. Main themes: trials, reviews, evidence synthesis. Best early reads: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review; Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury.
briefing.editorialHeadline: Regional Analgesia, Smarter Fluids, and Fewer Reflexes
briefing.editorialSummary:
A good chunk of today’s reading set is about backing away from reflex medicine and getting more precise about what actually changes outcomes. The standout practical trial may be the serratus anterior plane block paper: better rib fracture analgesia delivered safely by emergency physicians is the sort of result that can alter a shift tomorrow, not just a journal club next month. PRoMPT BOLUS pushes in a different direction, showing that the saline-versus-balanced crystalloid fight in pediatric septic shock may matter less than many of us have implied, while the TOWAR data does something similar for prehospital trauma transfusion by dampening the assumption that whole blood must outperform components.

The PE cluster is also worth attention. HI-PEITHO and the accompanying catheter-directed fibrinolysis commentary both sharpen the real question, which is not whether the technology exists but which intermediate-risk patients actually justify escalation beyond anticoagulation. Around the edges, there are several quieter but highly usable reads: a pediatric vein-locator trial with immediate procedural relevance, updated CAP guidance that supports a more selective antibiotic posture, and a poisoning guideline that reinforces the value of a toxidrome-first framework when the history is unreliable. Taken together, this is a useful editorial set because it rewards clinicians who are willing to replace habit with cleaner case selection, better technique, and more disciplined restraint.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between patient-to-nurse ratios in emergency departments and patient, staff, and organizational outcomes: a systematic review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use this as evidence for thoughtful acuity-aware staffing rather than as proof that one fixed ratio will solve ED safety. If you are arguing for service redesign, the stronger position is that staffing should track workload complexity, not just headcount.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review asks a policy-heavy but clinically relevant question: do explicit ED nurse staffing ratios improve outcomes for patients, staff, or departments?
dailyEditorial.summary: This review asks a policy-heavy but clinically relevant question: do explicit ED nurse staffing ratios improve outcomes for patients, staff, or departments? The available summary makes clear the evidence base is still patchy, with heterogeneous studies and no clean bedside message on mortality or flow. That matters because ratio debates are often treated as settled when the ED environment is much messier than ward medicine. The practical value here is less a new staffing number than a reminder that acuity, crowding, and task mix probably matter as much as raw patient counts.

dailyEditorial.title: Whole Blood Not Better Than Components En Route to Hospital After Traumatic Injury
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Early blood still matters; this just suggests whole blood may not outperform good component-based prehospital resuscitation. Protocol consistency, hemorrhage control, and rapid definitive care remain the real levers.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The TOWAR signal is useful because it cools some of the enthusiasm around prehospital whole blood as inherently superior for trauma resuscitation.
dailyEditorial.summary: The TOWAR signal is useful because it cools some of the enthusiasm around prehospital whole blood as inherently superior for trauma resuscitation. In patients at risk of hemorrhagic shock, whole blood and component therapy produced similar outcomes, which suggests the bigger win may be getting blood products moving early rather than obsessing over product purity. That does not make whole blood irrelevant, but it does make logistics and protocol reliability look more important than dogma. For most systems, this is a trial worth reading before rewriting transfusion pathways around presumed superiority.

dailyEditorial.title: HI-PEITHO Trial: Ultrasound-facilitated, Catheter Directed Fibrinolysis for PE
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Intermediate-risk PE still starts with anticoagulation and careful risk stratification. Consider catheter-based escalation only in the subset with meaningful deterioration risk and access to a team that can deliver it safely.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: HI-PEITHO keeps the intermediate-risk PE conversation alive without making anticoagulation any less central.
dailyEditorial.summary: HI-PEITHO keeps the intermediate-risk PE conversation alive without making anticoagulation any less central. Ultrasound-facilitated catheter-directed fibrinolysis appears to reduce short-term decompensation in selected higher-risk patients, but this is still a niche escalation strategy rather than a broad ED practice change. The interesting part is not whether a catheter can be placed, but which patients are sick enough to benefit before they crash and yet not so unstable that the decision is obvious. That tension is exactly why these data matter.

dailyEditorial.title: Effect of a liquid crystal vein locator on intravenous catheterization in young children: a randomized trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If your department has this device or a similar one, it looks like a reasonable adjunct for difficult pediatric cannulation. Better first-pass success is meaningful even when the intervention itself is low drama.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This is a very practical pediatric trial: a simple vein locator improved first-pass IV success, reduced puncture attempts, and shortened procedure time in young children.
dailyEditorial.summary: This is a very practical pediatric trial: a simple vein locator improved first-pass IV success, reduced puncture attempts, and shortened procedure time in young children. That is the sort of modest technological adjunct that can genuinely improve ED care when a distressed child and pressured team are both losing patience. The paper will not change resuscitation science, but it may change how quickly you get access on the next difficult toddler. Procedural efficiency matters when it reliably reduces sticks.

dailyEditorial.title: Serratus Anterior Plane Block for Acute Rib Fractures in the Emergency Department: A Randomized Controlled Trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If you already have ultrasound-guided regional anesthesia skills, SAPB looks like a credible ED tool for rib fractures rather than an ICU or anaesthesia-only move. The bedside question is increasingly about training and workflow, not proof of concept.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This randomized trial gives regional analgesia another nudge toward mainstream ED rib fracture care.
dailyEditorial.summary: This randomized trial gives regional analgesia another nudge toward mainstream ED rib fracture care. Adding serratus anterior plane block to usual care improved pain-related scores and was performed safely by emergency physicians, which is exactly the implementation question many departments care about. Rib fracture care often fails because we under-treat pain, then watch patients splint, retain secretions, and spiral. A scalable block that emergency clinicians can own is therefore more than a comfort intervention.

dailyEditorial.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Expect more justification for ultrasound-first thinking and shorter antibiotic courses in uncomplicated CAP. The practical shift is toward treating the patient’s risk profile, not reflexively treating every infiltrate the same way.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The updated CAP guidance is notable less for one headline change than for its steady push toward more selective, lighter-touch management.
dailyEditorial.summary: The updated CAP guidance is notable less for one headline change than for its steady push toward more selective, lighter-touch management. Lung ultrasound gets more legitimacy, viral-positive otherwise healthy patients may not automatically need antibiotics, and shorter antibiotic courses are increasingly acceptable in stable disease. None of that is radical in 2026, but it does help formalize a more restrained ED approach. For departments still practicing older broader-cover-longer habits, this is a worthwhile reset.

dailyEditorial.title: PRoMPT BOLUS: A Landmark PECARN Trial Defining Fluid Choice in Pediatric Sepsis
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For initial pediatric septic shock boluses, either balanced crystalloid or 0.9% saline appears reasonable on current evidence. Focus more on timely recognition, reassessment, and overall resuscitation quality than on winning the crystalloid culture war.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: PRoMPT BOLUS is the kind of large pragmatic sepsis trial that clarifies an argument without making fluid resuscitation simplistic.
dailyEditorial.summary: PRoMPT BOLUS is the kind of large pragmatic sepsis trial that clarifies an argument without making fluid resuscitation simplistic. In pediatric septic shock, balanced crystalloid and normal saline performed similarly across major outcomes, including kidney injury and mortality. That does not make fluid choice irrelevant, but it does take some heat out of a debate that often outran the evidence. The practical message is reassuring flexibility rather than a mandate to convert every protocol overnight.

dailyEditorial.title: Catheter-Directed Fibrinolysis for Pulmonary Embolism: Is It Old NEWS?
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Read this alongside the trial, not instead of it. The main value is in sharpening case selection and avoiding the slide from promising procedure to overused procedure.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This companion commentary on catheter-directed fibrinolysis matters because it checks enthusiasm against the reality of selected patients, specialized teams, and uncertain generalizability.
dailyEditorial.summary: This companion commentary on catheter-directed fibrinolysis matters because it checks enthusiasm against the reality of selected patients, specialized teams, and uncertain generalizability. Even when advanced PE interventions show signal, the bedside challenge is deciding whether the patient in front of you is sick enough to need escalation and stable enough to reach it. The commentary is worth pairing with HI-PEITHO because it pushes on those implementation questions rather than just admiring the technique.

dailyEditorial.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When the ingestion is unclear, build the case from physiology, likely toxidrome, and expected time course while getting poison-centre level advice early. A good general framework helps, but it should not delay specific toxicology consultation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Undifferentiated poisoning is exactly where clinicians benefit from a disciplined framework, and this RCEM/NPIS guideline leans into that.
dailyEditorial.summary: Undifferentiated poisoning is exactly where clinicians benefit from a disciplined framework, and this RCEM/NPIS guideline leans into that. The emphasis on toxidromes, toxicokinetics, early assessment, and supportive management is sensible because many poisoned patients arrive before the story is trustworthy. The key nuance is that this is a scaffold, not a replacement for agent-specific poison advice. As a piece of operational emergency medicine, it is probably more broadly useful than many single-study toxicology reads.

dailyEditorial.title: Systematic Review of Pediatric Ketamine in Emergency Department Procedural Sedation: Frequency and Predictors of Adverse Events
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Keep using ketamine thoughtfully, but use better pooled data when discussing risk and planning monitoring. Any predictor claims still need humility unless they are consistently supported across studies.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Ketamine remains a workhorse in pediatric ED sedation, so a large adverse-event review is immediately relevant even if it does not produce a dramatic new warning.
dailyEditorial.summary: Ketamine remains a workhorse in pediatric ED sedation, so a large adverse-event review is immediately relevant even if it does not produce a dramatic new warning. The strength here is aggregation: pooled event rates and a better sense of which proposed predictors actually deserve clinician attention. That should help departments counsel families, refine checklists, and calibrate how much weight to place on risk factors that are often repeated more confidently than they are proven. Expect this to be more protocol-shaping than practice-reversing.

dailyEditorial.title: Design Refinements for Sepsis Albumin Trials: Building on ICARUS-ED Experience
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Treat this as research infrastructure rather than bedside guidance. The immediate practical message is that early sepsis trial design is improving, not that albumin has earned a routine ED role.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This is less a sepsis practice changer than a trial-design note from the ICARUS-ED orbit.
dailyEditorial.summary: This is less a sepsis practice changer than a trial-design note from the ICARUS-ED orbit. Its value is in showing how difficult early ED sepsis intervention studies are to run well and where future albumin trials may need to tighten design, inclusion, and endpoints. For clinicians, the paper is interesting because it reminds us how often feasibility questions shape what evidence we eventually get. It is useful background if you follow sepsis fluids closely, but it should not be over-read as a mandate for albumin use.

dailyEditorial.title: Medication Safety Strategies for Geriatric Patients in the Emergency Department: A Narrative Review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Medication review in older adults should be an active intervention, not a documentation task. If you can hard-wire pharmacy support or prescribing prompts into discharge workflow, you will likely prevent more harm than with another generic frailty slogan.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Older adults continue to pay the price for fragmented medication histories, and this review makes that point without much ambiguity.
dailyEditorial.summary: Older adults continue to pay the price for fragmented medication histories, and this review makes that point without much ambiguity. The most actionable themes are thorough reconciliation, deliberate deprescribing of inappropriate agents, and building pharmacist or decision-support input into ED workflows. None of that is glamorous, but medication harm remains one of the most fixable drivers of poor geriatric outcomes after discharge. If your department wants a high-yield systems target, this is one.

dailyEditorial.title: TTL Tip 11: Be the TTL your prehospital teams want to hand over to
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If you lead trauma calls, build the handover before arrival: anticipate interventions, frame the priorities, and protect the first minute from chaos. ATMIST is only useful if the receiving team is actually ready to hear it.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This is FOAMed, but it addresses a real performance gap: poor trauma handovers waste time precisely when teams need shared situational awareness.
dailyEditorial.summary: This is FOAMed, but it addresses a real performance gap: poor trauma handovers waste time precisely when teams need shared situational awareness. The best point is that good handover starts before wheels hit the bay, with the trauma leader shaping the room, the roles, and the expected problems from the pre-alert. That sounds obvious, yet it is often the difference between an orderly reception and noisy improvisation. Operational advice like this travels well because it is immediately testable on shift.

dailyEditorial.title: A Randomized Controlled Trial of a Digital Patient Decision Tool to Increase Sexually Transmitted Infection Testing in the Emergency Department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Digital tools may help close testing gaps if they slot cleanly into registration or waiting-room flow. The right benchmark is not novelty but whether they improve uptake without creating new bottlenecks.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This trial sits in the growing category of digital nudges that try to improve preventive or public-health work inside the ED without overloading staff.
dailyEditorial.summary: This trial sits in the growing category of digital nudges that try to improve preventive or public-health work inside the ED without overloading staff. A patient-facing STI decision tool could matter if it increases testing uptake while preserving autonomy and workflow. The main question is less whether the concept is clever and more whether the effect size justifies implementation friction in a busy department. Still, this is the sort of intervention that can quietly outperform posters and vague clinician prompts.

dailyEditorial.title: Endovascular Thrombectomy Boosts Outcomes in Medium-Vessel Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not write off moderate-to-severe MeVO patients as automatically outside the thrombectomy conversation. Early specialist discussion and transfer logic may need to be more inclusive as this evidence matures.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Medium-vessel occlusion is starting to look less like a no-man’s-land for reperfusion decisions.
dailyEditorial.summary: Medium-vessel occlusion is starting to look less like a no-man’s-land for reperfusion decisions. The ORIENTAL-MeVO report suggests thrombectomy can improve functional independence in selected patients with meaningful deficits, extending the scope of who might benefit from advanced stroke pathways. As always, the exciting part is balanced by questions of external validity, service capability, and patient selection. But it is one more sign that stroke systems may keep widening beyond classic large-vessel targets.

dailyEditorial.title: A Woman With Rapidly Progressive Weakness
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Rapidly progressive weakness with autonomic involvement and a sensory level is a spinal emergency until proven otherwise. Prioritize localization, urgent imaging, and early specialty escalation rather than getting lost in broad weakness workups.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This clinical image-style case looks like a reminder of how quickly a spinal cord process can announce itself if you pay attention to pattern recognition.
dailyEditorial.summary: This clinical image-style case looks like a reminder of how quickly a spinal cord process can announce itself if you pay attention to pattern recognition. Rapid bilateral weakness, sphincter dysfunction, and a sensory level are the kind of constellation that should pull attention away from peripheral causes and toward urgent cord localization. The educational value is probably in the differential and the need for decisive imaging rather than in any exotic diagnosis. Cases like this are useful because they sharpen tempo-and-pattern instincts.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between prehospital time and trauma outcomes: A multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS)
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Shorter prehospital timelines are a worthy systems goal, but interpret time associations cautiously. The better question is which delays are modifiable without stripping away necessary on-scene care.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: PATOS reinforces the old truth that time matters in trauma, but also the equally important truth that sicker patients consume more time.
dailyEditorial.summary: PATOS reinforces the old truth that time matters in trauma, but also the equally important truth that sicker patients consume more time. Longer scene and resuscitation intervals tracked with worse outcomes, yet the authors appropriately flag confounding by severity. That means the paper is most useful for system reflection rather than simplistic stopwatch medicine. It supports streamlining prehospital care while resisting the lazy conclusion that every extra minute is avoidable error.

dailyEditorial.title: Major adverse cardiac events in patients with indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin testing and chest pain: a systematic review
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not over-interpret an isolated indeterminate hs-troponin. Serial testing, ECG context, and overall risk profile still do most of the real clinical work.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin results remain one of the more annoying modern chest pain problems, and this review confirms that the evidence around them is still messy.
dailyEditorial.summary: Indeterminate high-sensitivity troponin results remain one of the more annoying modern chest pain problems, and this review confirms that the evidence around them is still messy. Many patients in the literature did not even get serial testing, so it is hard to know what risk we are really measuring. That makes the paper less a rule-set and more a warning against false precision. In other words, an awkward troponin should push you back toward clinical assessment, not trick you into pretending the lab has settled the case.

dailyEditorial.title: High-Risk Medication Prescribing Among Older Adults in the Emergency Department: A National Assessment
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Improving geriatric prescribing is not just about the very old or obviously frail. Build discharge safeguards broadly, and check whether your own prescribing patterns mirror the subgroups flagged here.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: Nearly one in ten older adults filling a high-risk discharge prescription is still a substantial systems failure, even if the trend line is improving.
dailyEditorial.summary: Nearly one in ten older adults filling a high-risk discharge prescription is still a substantial systems failure, even if the trend line is improving. The signal that younger older adults and women were more exposed is useful because it identifies groups who may slip past generic geriatric safeguards. This is not an abstract prescribing-quality paper; it is a reminder that discharge is where a lot of medication harm gets exported out of the ED. There is enough here to justify targeted audit and feedback.

dailyEditorial.title: Civilian penetrating traumatic brain injury in South-East Norway
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Penetrating TBI should not trigger automatic therapeutic pessimism in salvageable patients. Early CTA, guideline-concordant neurocritical care, and prompt operative planning remain the basics worth getting right.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This series is valuable because it combines a grim injury pattern with a fairly practical message: survivors can do well enough that nihilism is a mistake, but care quality still depends on disciplined adherence to TBI…
dailyEditorial.summary: This series is valuable because it combines a grim injury pattern with a fairly practical message: survivors can do well enough that nihilism is a mistake, but care quality still depends on disciplined adherence to TBI principles. Lower-than-expected compliance with antibiotics, seizure prophylaxis, ICP monitoring, and timely revision highlights how easily even high-stakes trauma care drifts from guideline intent. The call for aggressive management in potential survivors feels justified. For trauma teams, this reads as both outcome data and a process audit.
